rawHead:
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Vertex operator algebras</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-begin -->

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Vertex ops 2 Axioms 3 Virasoro 4 Modular 5 $V^\natural$ 6 CFT & chiral

Vertex operator algebras

A vertex operator algebra is the algebraic shadow of two-dimensional conformal field theory: states are vectors, fields are formal Laurent series of operators indexed by states, and the only thing that replaces commutativity is locality — the requirement that any two fields commute up to a finite-order pole at the diagonal. This single relaxation is enough to encode the Virasoro algebra, force modular invariance on graded characters, and produce $V^\natural$, the algebra whose automorphism group is the Monster.

Prerequisites. Formal power series and $q$-expansions live in modular forms. The Lie-algebra background — root systems, the Cartan–Killing classification, and central extensions — comes from Lie algebras. Characters, irreducibles, and trace formulas are developed in representation theory. The downstream payoff is monstrous moonshine.

blocks.html:
1. Vertex operators and locality

Fix a $\mathbb{Z}$-graded complex vector space $V = \bigoplus_{n} V_n$. A vertex operator is a linear map $$Y(\,\cdot\,, z) \;:\; V \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{End}(V)[[z, z^{-1}]], \qquad Y(a, z) \;=\; \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} a_n\, z^{-n - 1},$$ that takes a state $a \in V$ to a formal Laurent series whose coefficients $a_n \in \mathrm{End}(V)$ are called the modes of $a$. The convention $z^{-n-1}$ (rather than $z^n$) is so that $a_n$ lowers degree by $n$, consistent with the conformal-weight grading we will introduce.

Two states $a, b$ produce two operator-valued series $Y(a, z)$ and $Y(b, w)$. They cannot in general commute as endomorphisms of $V$, because acting on a single fixed vector both have infinitely many non-zero modes — a single composition is itself a doubly infinite formal series. What replaces commutativity is

Locality. For every $a, b \in V$ there exists $N \ge 0$ (depending on $a, b$) such that

$$(z - w)^N \; \big[\, Y(a, z),\, Y(b, w) \,\big] \;=\; 0 \quad \text{in } \mathrm{End}(V)[[z^{\pm 1}, w^{\pm 1}]].$$

Multiplying by $(z - w)^N$ kills any pole of order at most $N$ on the diagonal $z = w$; the equation says that after this clearing-of-denominators the two compositions agree. Locality is strictly weaker than commutativity (it allows finite-order singularities at $z = w$) and strictly stronger than nothing (it forbids essential singularities).

Widget 1 · Mode bookkeeping
drag the slider to read off which mode $a_n$ comes from which power of $z$ in $Y(a,z) = \sum a_n z^{-n-1}$
n = −1
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const sl = $('#w-vop-n'), lb = $('#w-vop-nv'), svg = $('#w-vop-svg'), out = $('#w-vop-out');
  function render(){
    const n = +sl.value;
    lb.textContent = `n = ${n}`;
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:30,y1:120,x2:690,y2:120,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    // mode bins -4..4 mapped to z^{-(n+1)}
    const xs = [];
    for(let k=-4;k<=4;k++){
      const x = 60 + (k + 4) * 70;
      xs.push({k,x});
      const fill = (k===n) ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--panel2)';
      const stroke = (k===n) ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--line)';
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:x-22,y:90,width:44,height:30,rx:4,
        fill, stroke,'stroke-width':1.5}));
      const tlab = SVG('text',{x,y:111,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,
        fill:(k===n?'#111':'var(--ink)'),'font-weight':600});
      tlab.textContent = `a_${k}`;
      svg.appendChild(tlab);
      const tpow = SVG('text',{x,y:148,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,
        fill:'var(--mute)','font-style':'italic'});
      tpow.textContent = `z^{${-k-1}}`;
      svg.appendChild(tpow);
    }
    const ttl = SVG('text',{x:360,y:35,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':14,fill:'var(--ink)'});
    ttl.textContent = "Y(a, z) = … + a_{-2} z + a_{-1} + a_0 z^{-1} + a_1 z^{-2} + …";
    svg.appendChild(ttl);
    const ttl2 = SVG('text',{x:360,y:182,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    ttl2.textContent = "highlighted: coefficient of z^{-(n+1)}";
    svg.appendChild(ttl2);
    let s = `mode  a_${n}  is the coefficient of  z^{${-n-1}}\n`;
    if(n===-1) s += "this is the constant-in-z term; a_{-1} 1 = a (creation axiom)";
    else if(n>=0) s += "n ≥ 0 : these modes annihilate the vacuum (positive-energy half)";
    else s += "n ≤ -1 : these modes create from the vacuum (negative-energy half)";
    out.textContent = s;
  }
  sl.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Two further axioms tie the formal-series picture to a state space: the vacuum $\mathbf{1} \in V$ is a distinguished element with $Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}_V$, and creation demands $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1} \in V[[z]]$ with $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1}\big|_{z = 0} = a$. Reading the constant-in-$z$ coefficient gives $a_{-1}\mathbf{1} = a$: every state is recovered by acting with the $(-1)$-mode on the vacuum.

See also
  • Axioms and matrix examples
  • q-expansions and arithmetic data

See also
  • Axioms and matrix examples
  • q-expansions and arithmetic data

Used in
Vertex operator algebras · VOA axioms — vacuum, translation, locality, Jacobi
</section>

2. The full axiom system — vacuum, translation, locality, Jacobi

A vertex operator algebra is a quadruple $(V, Y, \mathbf{1}, \omega)$ where $V$ is a $\mathbb{Z}$-graded vector space (eventually $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ once a Virasoro is fixed), $Y : V \to \mathrm{End}(V)[[z^{\pm 1}]]$ is a state–field correspondence, $\mathbf{1} \in V$ is a vacuum vector, and $\omega \in V$ is a conformal vector (treated separately in §3). The axioms — there are five — split into two camps:

axiomstatementrole
Vacuum$Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}_V$$\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the operator algebra
Creation$Y(a, z)\mathbf{1} \in V[[z]]$, equal to $a$ at $z = 0$state–field correspondence is injective on states
Translation$[T, Y(a, z)] = \partial_z Y(a, z)$infinitesimal translation by an inner derivation
Locality$(z - w)^N [Y(a, z), Y(b, w)] = 0$ for $N \gg 0$fields commute up to a diagonal pole
Grading$V = \bigoplus_n V_n$ with $\dim V_n < \infty$, $V_n = 0$ for $n \ll 0$finiteness of the spectrum

An equivalent and often more useful repackaging is the Borcherds–Jacobi identity: for all $a, b, c \in V$ and all integers $\ell, m, n$, $$\sum_{i \ge 0} \binom{\ell}{i} (a_{m + i} b)_{n + \ell - i} c \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \binom{\ell}{i} \big( a_{m + \ell - i} b_{n + i} c \;-\; (-1)^\ell b_{n + \ell - i} a_{m + i} c \big).$$ The Jacobi identity bundles locality, weak associativity (the Goddard uniqueness theorem), and skew-symmetry into a single relation among modes. Once you accept it, all manipulations become combinatorics with binomial coefficients.

Widget 2 · Axiom dependency map
click a node to see which axioms force which structure
click an axiom node…
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#w-ax-svg'), out = $('#w-ax-out');
  const NODES = [
    {id:"vac",   x:120, y:60,  label:"Vacuum",     color:'var(--blue)',
     desc:"Y(1, z) = id_V. Forces 1 to be the unit; a_{-1} 1 = a recovers states from modes."},
    {id:"crea",  x:340, y:60,  label:"Creation",   color:'var(--blue)',
     desc:"Y(a, z) 1 ∈ V[[z]] with value a at z=0. Couples vacuum to grading: positive modes a_{n≥0} kill 1."},
    {id:"loc",   x:580, y:60,  label:"Locality",   color:'var(--cyan)',
     desc:"(z−w)^N [Y(a,z), Y(b,w)] = 0. Fields commute up to a finite-order pole on the diagonal — the OPE."},
    {id:"trans", x:230, y:200, label:"Translation",color:'var(--violet)',
     desc:"[T, Y(a,z)] = ∂_z Y(a,z). Infinitesimal translations are inner derivations of the operator algebra."},
    {id:"jac",   x:470, y:200, label:"Jacobi",     color:'var(--yellow)',
     desc:"Borcherds–Jacobi identity. Equivalent to locality + weak associativity; bundles everything into one relation."},
    {id:"gr",    x:350, y:280, label:"Grading",    color:'var(--green)',
     desc:"V = ⊕ V_n, finite-dim pieces, bounded below. Required so that traces and characters converge."},
  ];
  const EDGES = [
    ["vac","trans","↦"],["crea","trans","↦"],
    ["loc","jac","≡"], ["trans","jac","↦"],
    ["jac","gr","↦"], ["loc","gr","↦"]
  ];
  const NMAP = Object.fromEntries(NODES.map(n=>[n.id,n]));
  ensureArrow(svg,'arrow-ax','var(--mute)');
  EDGES.forEach(([a,b])=>{
    const A = NMAP[a], B = NMAP[b];
    drawArrow(svg, [A.x, A.y+18], [B.x, B.y-18], {color:'var(--mute)',markerId:'arrow-ax',width:1.2,curve:0});
  });
  NODES.forEach(n=>{
    const g = SVG('g',{style:'cursor:pointer'});
    g.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:n.x-58,y:n.y-18,width:116,height:36,rx:8,
      fill:'var(--panel2)',stroke:n.color,'stroke-width':1.5}));
    const t = SVG('text',{x:n.x,y:n.y+5,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':14,fill:'#fff','font-weight':600});
    t.textContent = n.label;
    g.appendChild(t);
    g.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
      out.textContent = `${n.label}\n${n.desc}`;
    });
    svg.appendChild(g);
  });
  // legend at viewport offset
  const L = SVG('g',{transform:'translate(540, 280)'});
  L.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:0,y:0,width:170,height:30,rx:4,
    fill:'rgba(0,0,0,0.4)',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
  const lt = SVG('text',{x:8,y:19,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  lt.textContent = "arrows: implication / equivalence";
  L.appendChild(lt);
  svg.appendChild(L);
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
The five axioms are not independent: in many treatments translation is derived from $L_{-1}$ (a Virasoro mode) and grading is derived from $L_0$, so the irreducible core is "Y plus vacuum plus locality plus a conformal vector". That is why the Borcherds–Jacobi identity has become the working definition.

See also
  • Lie algebra axioms — the locality axiom is to VOAs as the Jacobi identity is to Lie algebras.

Used in
Vertex operator algebras · Virasoro algebra and central charge
</section>

3. The Virasoro algebra and central charge

The conformal vector $\omega \in V$ is a state of conformal weight $2$ whose vertex operator $$Y(\omega, z) \;=\; \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} L_n\, z^{-n - 2}$$ generates a copy of the Virasoro algebra inside $\mathrm{End}(V)$: $$\boxed{\;[L_m, L_n] \;=\; (m - n)\, L_{m + n} \;+\; \frac{c}{12} (m^3 - m)\, \delta_{m + n,\, 0}\;}.$$ The constant $c \in \mathbb{C}$ is the central charge of the VOA; it is a numerical invariant rigidly determined by the VOA structure. The shift $-2$ in $z^{-n-2}$ encodes that $\omega$ has weight $2$: more generally a state of weight $h$ has $Y(a, z) = \sum_n a_n z^{-n - h}$.

Two further demands fix $\omega$'s role: $L_{-1} = T$ is the translation operator (so the translation axiom is now a consequence), and $L_0$ acts on $V_n$ by the integer $n$. The grading is therefore the spectrum of $L_0$.

Widget 3 · Virasoro bracket calculator
pick $m, n$ and central charge $c$; widget evaluates $[L_m, L_n]$ and shows the central term
c = 24
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const M = $('#w-vir-m'), N = $('#w-vir-n'), C = $('#w-vir-c'), CV = $('#w-vir-cv'), OUT = $('#w-vir-out');
  function render(){
    const m = parseInt(M.value, 10), n = parseInt(N.value, 10), c = parseInt(C.value, 10);
    CV.textContent = `c = ${c}`;
    const sum = m + n;
    const struct = m - n;
    const cocycle = (sum === 0) ? (m*m*m - m) : 0;
    const cterm = (sum === 0) ? (c * (m*m*m - m)) / 12 : 0;
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`[L_${m}, L_${n}]  =  ${struct} · L_${sum}` + (sum===0 ? `  +  (c/12)(m^3 − m) · δ_{m+n,0}` : ``));
    lines.push("");
    lines.push(`structure constant  (m − n)            = ${struct}`);
    lines.push(`m^3 − m                               = ${m*m*m - m}`);
    lines.push(`(m^3 − m) · δ_{m+n,0}                = ${cocycle}`);
    lines.push(`central term  (c/12)(m^3 − m) δ_{m+n,0} = ${cterm}`);
    if(sum !== 0){
      lines.push("");
      lines.push("(central term vanishes when m + n ≠ 0)");
    } else if(m === -1 || m === 0 || m === 1){
      lines.push("");
      lines.push("(central term vanishes on the sl_2 subalgebra ⟨L_{−1}, L_0, L_1⟩ since m^3 − m = 0)");
    }
    OUT.textContent = lines.join("\n");
  }
  [M, N, C].forEach(el => el.addEventListener('input', render));
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Among VOAs the central charge takes integer or rational values for the most-studied examples: $c = 1$ for the Heisenberg (free-boson) VOA, $c = \dim L$ for a lattice VOA on a rank-$\dim L$ lattice, $c = \frac{k\dim\mathfrak{g}}{k + h^\vee}$ at level $k$ for the affine Kac–Moody VOA $\widehat{\mathfrak{g}}_k$, and $c = 24$ for the moonshine module $V^\natural$. Unitary VOAs satisfy $c \ge 0$.

See also
  • Cartan–Killing classification — the Virasoro algebra is the unique non-trivial central extension of the Witt algebra; central extensions belong to the deformation-theoretic side of Lie theory.
  • Cartan, Killing, and semisimplicity

Used in
Vertex operator algebras · Modular invariance — Zhu's theorem
</section>

4. Modular invariance — Zhu's theorem

If $V$ is a "nice enough" VOA — rational and $C_2$-cofinite, in the technical jargon — it has only finitely many irreducible modules $M_1, \ldots, M_r$. To each module $M$ attach the graded character $$\mathrm{ch}_M(\tau) \;=\; \tr_M\, q^{L_0 - c/24}, \qquad q = e^{2\pi i \tau},$$ treated as a formal $q$-series. The $-c/24$ shift is the Casimir of the Virasoro vacuum representation; equivalently it is the shift that appears in $\eta(\tau) = q^{1/24}\prod (1 - q^n)$.

Zhu's theorem (1996). The vector space spanned by $\mathrm{ch}_{M_1}, \ldots, \mathrm{ch}_{M_r}$ is invariant under the action of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ via $\tau \mapsto \frac{a\tau + b}{c\tau + d}$. Each character $\mathrm{ch}_{M_i}$ is one component of a vector-valued modular form for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$.

Two consequences are immediate. First, characters need not individually be $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-modular — they merely span an $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-stable space. Second, the matrix $S$ representing the action of $\tau \mapsto -1/\tau$ on the basis of characters carries the fusion-rule data of the VOA via the Verlinde formula. Modular invariance is the bridge from the algebraic VOA side to the analytic modular-forms side.

Widget 4 · Character of $V^\natural$ — leading $q$-coefficients
slide $N$ to read off $\mathrm{ch}_{V^\natural}(\tau) = \sum (\dim V^\natural_n) q^{n - 1}$ and recover $J(\tau)$
N = 3
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  // dim V^natural_n = c_n (Fourier coef of j(tau))
  // grading shift: in V^natural, V^natural_n has L_0 - c/24 = n - 1 (since c = 24, c/24 = 1)
  // so ch_{V^natural}(tau) = sum_n (dim V^natural_n) q^{n-1}, leading term q^{-1}.
  const C = ["1","196884","21493760","864299970","20245856256","333202640600","4252023300096"];
  const sl = $('#w-zhu-N'), lb = $('#w-zhu-Nv'), out = $('#w-zhu-out');
  function render(){
    const N = +sl.value;
    lb.textContent = `N = ${N}`;
    let lines = [];
    lines.push("ch_{V^natural}(tau)  =  tr q^{L_0 − c/24}    (c = 24, so c/24 = 1)");
    lines.push("");
    lines.push("n   L_0 − c/24    dim V^natural_n");
    lines.push("--  -----------   ---------------");
    for(let n=0;n<=N;n++){
      const exp = (n - 1).toString().padStart(3," ");
      const dim = C[n].padStart(15," ");
      lines.push(`${n.toString().padStart(2," ")}      ${exp}        ${dim}`);
    }
    lines.push("");
    let series = "ch = q^{-1}";
    for(let n=1;n<=N;n++){
      const e = n - 1;
      const term = (e === 0) ? `${C[n]}` : `${C[n]}·q^{${e}}`;
      series += `  +  ${term}`;
    }
    series += "  +  …";
    lines.push(series);
    lines.push("");
    lines.push("compare j(tau) − 744 = J(tau) = q^{-1} + 196884·q + 21493760·q^2 + …");
    out.textContent = lines.join("\n");
  }
  sl.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The smallest unitary rational VOAs at $c < 1$ are the minimal-model VOAs $\mathrm{Vir}(p, q)$ at central charge $c = 1 - 6(p - q)^2 / pq$; their characters are the famous Rogers–Ramanujan-type identities. At $c = 1$ the Heisenberg VOA gives $\mathrm{ch} = 1/\eta(\tau)$, with the modular weight $-1/2$ visible in the $\eta$-shift. At $c = 24$ the moonshine module gives $\mathrm{ch} = J(\tau)$ — a weight-$0$ modular function.

See also
  • Modular form definition — Zhu's theorem promotes characters to vector-valued modular forms.

Used in
Vertex operator algebras · Applications — chiral CFT and chiral algebras
Vertex operator algebras · The moonshine module $V^\natural$
</section>

5. The moonshine module $V^\natural$

Frenkel, Lepowsky, and Meurman built $V^\natural$ in the mid-1980s by orbifolding the lattice VOA on the Leech lattice $\Lambda$. The construction has three layers:

stepobjectgraded dim
1$V_\Lambda$ — the lattice VOA on the Leech lattice $\Lambda$ (rank $24$)$\Theta_\Lambda(\tau) / \eta(\tau)^{24}$
2$V_\Lambda^+$ — fixed points of the $-1$ involution (a VOA itself)$\frac{1}{2}\big(\mathrm{ch}\, V_\Lambda + \mathrm{ch}\, V_\Lambda^{tw}\big)$
3$V^\natural = V_\Lambda^+ \oplus V_\Lambda^{tw,+}$ — the $\mathbb{Z}/2$ orbifold$J(\tau) = j(\tau) - 744$

The miracle is that the orbifolding kills the constant term ($744$) and leaves a graded VOA whose graded dimension is exactly $J(\tau)$. By construction the Monster $\mathbb{M}$ acts as automorphisms of $V^\natural$ — and FLM showed it is the full automorphism group.

Widget 5 · McKay–Thompson series viewer
pick a Monster conjugacy class; widget shows $T_g(\tau) = \sum \tr(g\,|\,V^\natural_n)\, q^{n-1}$ leading coefficients and its Hauptmodul label
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  // McKay-Thompson series leading traces, from Conway-Norton tables.
  // Format: name, Gamma label (Hauptmodul of), q^-1 coef, q coef, q^2 coef, q^3 coef.
  const T = {
    "1A": {g:"Γ_0(1)+",       q:[1,196884, 21493760,    864299970]},
    "2A": {g:"Γ_0(2)+",       q:[1,4372,   96256,       1240002]},
    "2B": {g:"Γ_0(2)",        q:[1,276,    -2048,       11202]},
    "3A": {g:"Γ_0(3)+",       q:[1,783,    8672,        65367]},
    "3B": {g:"Γ_0(3)",        q:[1,54,     -76,         243]},
    "5A": {g:"Γ_0(5)+",       q:[1,134,    760,         3345]},
  };
  const S = $('#w-moon-g'), OUT = $('#w-moon-out');
  function render(){
    const g = S.value;
    const data = T[g];
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`McKay–Thompson series  T_${g}(tau)  =  Σ tr(g | V^natural_n) q^{n-1}`);
    lines.push("");
    lines.push(`leading coefficients (n = 0, 1, 2, 3):`);
    lines.push(`q^{-1}  :  ${data.q[0]}        (always 1 — vacuum is g-fixed)`);
    lines.push(`q^{0}   :  ${data.q[1]}`);
    lines.push(`q^{1}   :  ${data.q[2]}`);
    lines.push(`q^{2}   :  ${data.q[3]}`);
    lines.push("");
    lines.push(`Conway–Norton: T_${g}(tau) is the Hauptmodul of  ${data.g}`);
    lines.push(`(i.e., generates the function field of a genus-zero modular curve)`);
    OUT.textContent = lines.join("\n");
  }
  S.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The graded dimension is the case $g = 1$, and recovers $J(\tau)$. For $g \ne 1$ the trace $\tr(g \mid V^\natural_n)$ assembles into a different $q$-series — Conway and Norton conjectured (and Borcherds proved) that each such series is the Hauptmodul of a genus-zero subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ commensurable with $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$. Borcherds' proof routes through the Monster Lie algebra, a generalised Kac–Moody algebra obtained by tensoring $V^\natural$ with the rank-2 Lorentzian-lattice VOA and applying the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem; its denominator identity gives recursion relations among the trace coefficients that uniquely pin them to the Hauptmodul side.

See also
  • Borcherds' proof — generalized Kac–Moody and replication — the Monster Lie algebra is built from $V^\natural$.
  • Characters and orthogonality — McKay–Thompson series are graded characters of the Monster acting on $V^\natural$.
</section>

6. Applications — chiral CFT and Beilinson–Drinfeld chiral algebras

Vertex operator algebras axiomatise the chiral half of two-dimensional conformal field theory. A 2D CFT factors (subject to modular invariance) into a holomorphic and an antiholomorphic sector; each sector is a VOA. The full theory is reconstructed from the two halves plus gluing data, and modular invariance of the partition function is exactly Zhu's theorem applied to both halves.

The geometric upgrade is due to Beilinson and Drinfeld: a chiral algebra on an algebraic curve $X$ is a $\mathcal{D}_X$-module $\mathcal{A}$ together with a chiral product $$\mu : j_*j^* (\mathcal{A} \boxtimes \mathcal{A}) \;\longrightarrow\; \Delta_*\mathcal{A}$$ (where $j$ is the open immersion of $X^2 \setminus \Delta$ into $X^2$ and $\Delta$ is the diagonal) satisfying associativity and skew-symmetry constraints. Restricting to the formal disc near a closed point recovers a VOA; varying the point globalises it to the entire curve.

Widget 6 · VOA construction zoo
pick a construction; widget shows the input data and central charge $c$
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const ZOO = {
    heis: {
      name: "Heisenberg (free boson)",
      input: "rank-1 abelian Lie algebra h with non-degenerate form",
      c: "c = 1 (rank 1)",
      detail: "Generators b_n with [b_m, b_n] = m δ_{m+n,0}; Y(b, z) = sum b_n z^{-n-1}.\nCharacter: ch = 1/eta(tau).\nThe simplest interesting VOA — the building block for lattice and affine constructions."
    },
    lat: {
      name: "Lattice VOA V_L",
      input: "even positive-definite lattice L of rank r",
      c: "c = r = rank L",
      detail: "Underlying space C[L] tensor S(h_-) where h = L tensor C, h_- the negative Heisenberg modes.\nVertex operators e^{alpha} for alpha in L are the lattice exponentials.\nL = Leech (rank 24): central charge 24, building block of the moonshine module."
    },
    aff: {
      name: "Affine Kac–Moody VOA  V_k(g)",
      input: "simple Lie algebra g, level k (positive integer)",
      c: "c = k·dim(g) / (k + h^∨)",
      detail: "Vacuum module of the affine Lie algebra hat{g} at level k, modulo the singular vectors at integral level.\nIrreducible modules ↔ integrable highest-weight reps of hat{g}_k.\nUnderlies the Wess–Zumino–Witten model in CFT."
    },
    vir: {
      name: "Virasoro minimal model  Vir(p, q)",
      input: "coprime integers p, q ≥ 2",
      c: "c = 1 − 6(p−q)^2 / (p·q)",
      detail: "(p, q) = (3, 4): Ising model, c = 1/2.\n(p, q) = (4, 5): tricritical Ising, c = 7/10.\nFinitely many irreducible modules — characters give the famous Rogers–Ramanujan-type identities."
    },
    vnat: {
      name: "Moonshine module  V^natural",
      input: "Z/2-orbifold of V_Λ for Λ = Leech lattice",
      c: "c = 24",
      detail: "Graded dimension = J(tau) = j(tau) − 744.\nAutomorphism group = the Monster M (full Aut group, FLM 1988).\nMcKay–Thompson series tr(g|V^natural_n) → Hauptmoduln (Borcherds 1992)."
    },
    bd: {
      name: "Beilinson–Drinfeld chiral algebra",
      input: "a smooth algebraic curve X, plus a D_X-module with chiral product",
      c: "c is intrinsic to the local VOA; global object lives over X",
      detail: "A chiral algebra is the global / curve-valued upgrade of a VOA.\nRestriction to formal disc at x ∈ X recovers a VOA.\nFundamental object in geometric Langlands: the BD-affine grassmannian and BD chiral chiral algebras give the Hecke side."
    }
  };
  const S = $('#w-app-k'), OUT = $('#w-app-out');
  function render(){
    const d = ZOO[S.value];
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`construction: ${d.name}`);
    lines.push(`input data:   ${d.input}`);
    lines.push(`central charge: ${d.c}`);
    lines.push("");
    lines.push(d.detail);
    OUT.textContent = lines.join("\n");
  }
  S.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The chiral-algebra framework is the technical heart of the geometric Langlands program: the Hecke side is built from BD chiral algebras on the affine Grassmannian, and the Galois side from $\mathcal{D}$-modules on the moduli stack of bundles. The "geometric Satake" equivalence is, in this language, a statement that two chiral algebras are equivalent. None of this would have been formulable without the VOA prehistory — locality, modular invariance, and rationality are the formal-power-series shadows of an entire chapter of representation theory.

See also
  • Group representations — VOA modules form a modular tensor category, the natural home for fusion rules.
</section>
